children.name: Atrax robustus
children.description: Widely considered the world's most dangerous spider. Large and aggressive; males 5x more toxic than females. Delta-atracotoxin lethal to primates. No deaths recorded since antivenom introduction in 1981.

children.name: Atrax sutherlandi
children.description: A large funnel-web of southern NSW and Victoria; named after toxinologist Struan Sutherland, who developed the funnel-web antivenom.

children.name: Atrax yorkmainorum
children.description: A funnel-web of the Australian Capital Territory region; described from the woodlands around Canberra.

children.name: Hadronyche versuta
children.description: Arboreal; found in NSW forests. Less aggressive than Atrax robustus but similarly potent venom.

children.name: Hadronyche formidabilis
children.description: Largest funnel-web spider; restricted to Queensland rainforests. Leg span up to 8cm.

children.name: Hadronyche cerberea
children.description: An arboreal funnel-web of NSW that nests in tree hollows; venom is medically significant and has caused severe envenomations.

children.name: Hadronyche infensa
children.description: A ground-dwelling funnel-web of Queensland and northern NSW; its venom is a leading source of insecticidal peptide research.

children.name: Hadronyche modesta
children.description: A smaller funnel-web of Victoria and South Australia; one of the southernmost members of the family.

children.name: Hadronyche meridiana
children.description: A funnel-web found around Adelaide and the Mount Lofty Ranges in South Australia.

children.name: Hadronyche venenata
children.description: The funnel-web species of Tasmania; lives in moist forest litter and under logs.

children.name: Hadronyche pulvinator
children.description: A small funnel-web associated with caves and rocky crevices in Tasmania.

children.name: Hadronyche valida
children.description: A funnel-web of the Atherton Tableland rainforests in tropical Queensland.

children.name: Hadronyche anzses
children.description: A tropical funnel-web described from the rainforests of far north Queensland.

children.name: Hadronyche nimoola
children.description: A funnel-web of upland forest in southeastern Queensland and northern NSW.

children.name: Hadronyche macquariensis
children.description: A funnel-web of coastal forests around Port Macquarie in NSW.

children.name: Hadronyche flindersi
children.description: A funnel-web of the Flinders Ranges and adjacent uplands in South Australia.

children.name: Hadronyche eyrei
children.description: A funnel-web described from the Eyre Peninsula region of South Australia.

children.name: Hadronyche adelaidensis
children.description: A small ground-dwelling funnel-web of the Adelaide Hills.

children.name: Hadronyche pilbara
children.description: An arid-zone funnel-web from the Pilbara region of Western Australia, unusual for the family's typically wetter range.

children.name: Hadronyche levisi
children.description: A funnel-web of the Hunter region of NSW; named after arachnologist Herbert Walter Levi.

children.name: Hadronyche elegans
children.description: A slender arboreal funnel-web of coastal NSW rainforest.

children.name: Hadronyche lamingtonensis
children.description: A funnel-web of the cool montane rainforest of Lamington National Park in Queensland.

children.name: Hadronyche montana
children.description: A montane funnel-web of the upper Blue Mountains of NSW, found under logs and in damp banks.

children.name: Hadronyche hirsutipes
children.description: A funnel-web of Victoria and southern NSW, distinguished by the notably dense scopulae (hair tufts) on the tarsi that give the species its name. Like most Hadronyche it is a ground-dweller in moist forest and heathland, constructing a silk-lined burrow with radiating trip-lines. Medically significant but rarer in urban settings than Atrax robustus.

children.name: Hadronyche karawatha
children.description: A funnel-web described from Karawatha Forest in the southern suburbs of Brisbane, Queensland — one of the few remaining patches of coastal heath and open eucalypt forest in the greater Brisbane metropolitan region. Its presence in this heavily urbanised landscape makes it an important species for public health awareness. Like other southeast Queensland Hadronyche it constructs a burrow in clay soils with a silk collar.

children.name: Hadronyche orichalcea
children.description: One of the earlier-described Hadronyche from southeastern Australia, named for the brassy, ochre-tinged colouration of its carapace and legs — orichalcea means 'brass-coloured' in Latin. Found in moist sclerophyll forest in NSW and Victoria. A medium-sized ground-dwelling funnel-web that builds burrows under logs, rocks and in soil banks in shaded gullies.

children.name: Hadronyche jenningsi
children.description: A funnel-web of upland rainforest and wet sclerophyll forest in southern Queensland, described from specimens collected in montane habitat on the Queensland–NSW border ranges. Like most Hadronyche it builds a retreating burrow lined with silk and fitted with a collar and trip-lines.

children.name: Hadronyche claudiae
children.description: A funnel-web described from coastal and hinterland forests of NSW, one of several Hadronyche species recognized from the highly diverse spider fauna of the New South Wales coast ranges. Medium-sized; inhabits moist forest gullies where it builds silk-lined burrows under bark, in rotting logs, or in soft clay banks.

children.name: Hadronyche raveni
children.description: A funnel-web named in honour of Robert Raven, Senior Curator of Arachnology at the Queensland Museum and one of the world's leading authorities on Australian spiders. Described from Queensland rainforest, it contributes to the remarkable diversity of Hadronyche in the wet tropics and subtropics of northeastern Australia where dense rainforest provides ideal habitat for these ground-dwelling or arboreal funnel-webs.

children.name: Hadronyche hercules
children.description: A large funnel-web of tropical and subtropical Queensland, named for its robust build — among the largest Hadronyche species after the closely related H. formidabilis. Found in rainforest and wet sclerophyll forest of northeastern Queensland, where it builds substantial silk-lined burrows. Like H. formidabilis it inhabits tree hollows and root cavities in wetter forest types as well as the ground layer.

children.name: Hadronyche alpina
children.description: A funnel-web of the alpine and subalpine zones of the Australian Alps in Victoria and southern NSW, one of the highest-altitude members of the family. Adapted to cooler temperatures and shorter active seasons than lowland relatives, building burrows in rocky soil and under alpine herb-field vegetation above the treeline. Its ability to function in near-freezing temperatures distinguishes it physiologically from most Australian funnel-webs.

children.name: Hadronyche barrigae
children.description: A funnel-web described from the forests of New South Wales and southern Queensland, one of several Hadronyche species identified through systematic survey work on the Atracidae of eastern Australia in recent decades. Ground-dwelling; builds a characteristic funnel-web burrow with radiating signal threads in moist forest soil.

children.name: Hadronyche maculata
children.description: A funnel-web distinguished by lighter spotting or mottling on the abdomen, in contrast to the typically uniform dark colouration of most Hadronyche. Found in moist forest of eastern Australia. Like all members of the genus, females tend to remain in or near the burrow while males wander extensively during the autumn mating season, which accounts for most human encounters with the family.

children.name: Illawarra wisharti
children.description: A funnel-web of the Illawarra coast of NSW; the type species of a genus split from Hadronyche in 2010.
